title: Detect the leak
prompt_md:
## Goal

A raw owning pointer is easy to leak. Build a class `IntBuffer` that takes
sole ownership of a heap-allocated `int` array and guarantees it is released
exactly once, by the owner, when the owner goes out of scope. The class also
provides two read operations over the stored values.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 100), the number of values.
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers (-10000 <= each <= 10000).

## Output

- Line 1: `acquired <n>` -- printed when the buffer takes ownership of its memory.
- Line 2: `sum <s>` -- the sum of the stored values.
- Line 3: `largest <m>` -- the largest stored value.
- Line 4: `released <n>` -- printed when the buffer frees its memory.

## Notes

The buffer must own its array (allocate with `new[]`, free with `delete[]`),
and lines 1 and 4 must be produced as a direct consequence of construction and
destruction -- not printed by hand from `main`. Do not leak.

title: Ownership transfer function
prompt_md:
## Goal

A function can produce a heap allocation and hand ownership of it back to the
caller, who then becomes responsible for freeing it. Write a function that
builds a brand-new array of **running sums** of its input and returns ownership
of that array (bundled with its size). The caller prints the result and frees it.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 100).
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers (-10000 <= each <= 10000).

## Output

- Line 1: the running sums, space-separated. The i-th running sum is the sum of
  the first `i + 1` input values.
- Line 2: `cleaned up`

## Notes

Use the provided `OwnedArray` struct (`int* data; int size;`) to carry the
pointer and size out of the function. The returned array must be a separate
allocation owned by the caller. Free everything you allocate.
